summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier post about Wavelength, a group messaging app with AI integration. He compiles reader feedback, noting the most-requested features (reactions, message editing, search) and the challenge of group discoverability given the app's privacy-first design. He highlights several notable responses including a Dithering podcast episode and media coverage. The most interesting observation is his puzzlement that Windows users haven't complained about the lack of a Windows app, while the absence of an Android app draws constant attention.
key_points: Top missing Wavelength features according to users: reactions/tapbacks, message editing, and search
Wavelength's privacy focus creates a group discoverability problem with no public directory
The lack of an Android app draws repeated complaints, but the absence of a Windows app has gone entirely unremarked
AI chat in a group messaging context is proving fun and engaging based on early user experiences
money_quotes: A side effect of Wavelength's focus on privacy is that there's no public directory of groups.
I really can't imagine spending much time using any messaging platform that doesn't have a Mac client, or at the very least a web app.
Less obvious and very curious to me is this: I haven't seen a single complaint about the fact that there is no Windows app, nor any announced plans to create one.
But Windows users are OK with that?
I suspect this will sort itself out as Wavelength gains traction, but in the meantime, it can seem a bit quiet if you don't know many other people using Wavelength.
key_insight: The asymmetry between Android users demanding a native app and Windows users' apparent indifference reveals how differently mobile and desktop platforms factor into modern messaging expectations.
